title: Valuation — Estimating What a Company Is Worth
objectives: Apply comparable company analysis to value a NZ share
Use a simplified earnings-based valuation to estimate a target price
Identify the assumptions and uncertainty in any valuation exercise

vocabulary.definition: An analyst's estimate of where a share price should trade in the next 12 months, based on valuation methodology. Not a guarantee — an estimate based on assumptions.
Forecast EPS multiplied by an appropriate P/E multiple. If EPS is expected to be $0.60 and a 20× P/E is appropriate, target price = $0.60 × 20 = $12.00.
Valuing a company by valuing each of its business divisions separately, then adding them together. Useful for conglomerates like Infratil that own multiple distinct businesses.
When a share's market price is below the analyst's estimated intrinsic value. The discount provides a margin of safety.
When a share trades above estimated intrinsic value. Buying at a premium means paying more than fair value — higher risk if the premium is unjustified.

activities.title: The Simplest Valuation Method — P/E Based Price Target
activities.notePrompt: Estimate a 12-month price target for Spark NZ: Forecast EPS $0.23, appropriate P/E based on peers is 18–20×. Calculate the target price range.
activities.content:
The most practical valuation approach for ordinary investors is the earnings-based price target.

Formula: Target price = Forecast EPS × Appropriate P/E multiple

Step 1 — Estimate next year's EPS:
Use the current EPS and apply a growth rate. If Spark's current EPS is $0.219 and you expect 5% growth: Forecast EPS = $0.219 × 1.05 = $0.230.

Step 2 — Choose an appropriate P/E multiple:
Look at: (a) the company's own historical P/E average, (b) peer companies in the same sector, (c) broader market P/E context.
For Spark, NZ telco peers trade at 17–22× earnings. Given Spark's moderate growth prospects and high yield, 18–20× seems appropriate.

Step 3 — Calculate target price range:
Low: $0.230 × 18 = $4.14
High: $0.230 × 20 = $4.60
Target price range: $4.14 – $4.60

Compare to current price:
Current price: $4.18. Target range implies modest upside (0% to +10%).

This is a one-year forward view — imprecise but directionally useful. If the share currently trades at $4.18 and your fundamental analysis suggests $4.14–$4.60 is fair, you might conclude it is roughly fairly valued and look elsewhere for better opportunity.

Important caveat: All assumptions in this model are uncertain. A change in interest rates, a profit warning from management, or a change in sector sentiment can invalidate the analysis quickly. Target prices should always be presented as ranges, not point estimates.

activities.title: Comparable Company Analysis — What Are Peers Worth?
activities.notePrompt: Given the peer comparison table, calculate Contact Energy's implied target price range if it should trade at the NZ utilities sector average P/E of 22–25×. Contact's forecast EPS is $0.38.
activities.content:
Comparable company analysis ('comps') values a company by reference to how similar companies are priced by the market.

NZ utilities sector P/E comparison:
Meridian Energy: P/E 28× | ROE 14% | Dividend yield 3.6% | Renewable-only generator
Genesis Energy: P/E 18× | ROE 11% | Dividend yield 6.2% | Mix of renewable and gas generation
Contact Energy: P/E 23× | ROE 10% | Dividend yield 4.6% | Geothermal + gas + hydro
Sector average P/E: approximately 23×

Applying comps to Contact Energy:
If Contact Energy should trade at 22–25× P/E (sector range, excluding premium Meridian)
Forecast EPS: $0.38 (5% growth from current $0.360)
Target price: $0.38 × 22 = $8.36 low; $0.38 × 25 = $9.50 high
Current price: $8.35

Conclusion: At $8.35, Contact Energy appears roughly fairly valued to slightly discounted compared to its sector peers. The 23× sector average implies an $8.74 target — about 4.7% upside from current price. Not cheap enough to be compelling, but not expensive either.

Key variable: If you believe interest rates will fall significantly (which improves utility valuations), you might assign a higher appropriate P/E (say 27×) giving a $10.26 target — implying 23% upside. The sensitivity of valuation to P/E assumptions illustrates why multiple scenarios are important.

activities.statement: A price target calculated using forecast EPS multiplied by an appropriate P/E ratio is a precise prediction of where the share price will be in 12 months.
activities.explanation: False. Price targets are estimates based on assumptions — forecast EPS (which can be wrong), an appropriate P/E multiple (subjective and dependent on many factors), and time horizon. Professional analysts' price targets are wrong more often than right over 12 months. The value of the exercise is not the precise number but the framework: it forces you to be explicit about growth assumptions and the multiple you are willing to pay, then assess whether the current price is above or below that estimate.

activities.title: Margin of Safety — The Most Important Valuation Concept
activities.notePrompt: If your valuation of FPH is $24.00 fair value, at what price would you buy with a 20% margin of safety? At what price would you sell if it reaches fair value?
activities.content:
Benjamin Graham — Warren Buffett's mentor — introduced the concept of the margin of safety: the difference between estimated intrinsic value and the price you pay. The larger the gap, the more protection you have against being wrong.

Why margin of safety matters:
Every valuation is based on assumptions that could prove wrong. If you estimate a company is worth $25 and buy at $24.50, you have almost no buffer — if your EPS estimate is 5% too optimistic, the fair value falls to $23.75 and you already have a loss.

If you buy at $20 (20% discount to $25 fair value), you can be 20% wrong on your assumptions and still approximately break even.

Math example — FPH:
Estimated intrinsic value: $24.00
20% margin of safety: buy at $24 × (1 − 0.20) = $19.20 or below
Current price: $21.40 — trading 10.8% above the margin of safety entry point

Conclusion: FPH is too expensive for a value investor seeking a 20% margin of safety. A patient investor might watch for a market correction or FPH-specific weakness to bring it to $19.20.

Moments to find margin of safety: market-wide corrections, sector-specific sell-offs, company-specific bad news that is temporary rather than structural (supply chain disruption, one-off costs), or simply when a quality business is overlooked by the market.

What happens when you buy with a large margin of safety: even if you are somewhat wrong on the business, the low entry price protects you. And if you are right, the return is exceptional.

questions.question: You estimate Mainfreight's fair value at $80 per share based on forecast EPS of $3.20 and a 25× P/E multiple. The current price is $68.50. What is the discount to fair value and would a 15% margin of safety investor buy?
options: 14.4% discount to fair value. With a 15% margin of safety requiring purchase below $68 ($80 × 0.85), the investor would not quite buy at $68.50 — just above their entry point
17% discount to fair value. A 15% margin of safety requirement is satisfied at $68.50 since the discount exceeds 15%
14.4% discount. A 15% margin of safety requires buying at $68 or below ($80 × 0.85 = $68). At $68.50, the investor is just above their threshold and should wait
14.4% discount. Any discount above 10% satisfies any margin of safety threshold
questions.explanation: Discount to fair value: ($80 − $68.50) ÷ $80 × 100 = 14.4%. A 15% margin of safety requires buying at $80 × (1 − 0.15) = $80 × 0.85 = $68.00 or below. At $68.50, the investor is 50 cents above their entry threshold — just slightly too expensive for a strict 15% margin of safety buyer. They would wait for the price to reach $68.00 or set a limit order there.

questions.question: Two analysts value Spark NZ at different prices: Analyst A says $4.60 (20× P/E on $0.23 EPS). Analyst B says $4.14 (18× P/E on $0.23 EPS). What is the most likely reason for the different valuations?
options: One analyst has inside information about Spark's earnings the other does not
The EPS forecasts are different — Analyst A uses a higher growth assumption

They have used different P/E multiples — Analyst A believes 20× is the appropriate multiple for Spark's growth and quality profile, while Analyst B considers 18× more appropriate given competitive pressures on the telco sector
Analyst B has made a calculation error — there is only one correct P/E for each company
questions.explanation: Both analysts used the same EPS estimate ($0.23) but different P/E multiples (20× vs 18×). Choosing an appropriate P/E multiple is the most subjective and variable part of earnings-based valuation. A more optimistic view of Spark's competitive resilience, dividend sustainability and sector dynamics might justify 20×; a more cautious view of its narrowing moat and competitive pressure might argue for 18×. Neither is definitively right — both are defensible with different underlying assumptions.

activities.title: Overvalued, Fairly Valued or Undervalued? Sort These Scenarios

pairs.item: Fair value estimate $20. Current price $14. 30% discount — well below margin of safety entry.
Fair value estimate $15. Current price $18. 20% above fair value with no margin of safety.
Fair value estimate $25. Current price $24.50. Trading at 2% discount — essentially at fair value.
Forecast EPS $0.40, sector P/E 20×, implied fair value $8. Current price $6 — 25% below fair value.
Forecast EPS $0.55, appropriate P/E 18×, implied value $9.90. Current price $11.50 — trading at premium.

activities.explanation: Valuation is always relative to your estimate of fair value — which is itself based on assumptions. The margin of safety concept acknowledges that you will sometimes be wrong. By demanding a discount to fair value before buying, you protect against errors in your own analysis.

activities.statement: Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) valuation is most useful for companies that operate as a single focused business in one industry.
activities.explanation: False. SOTP is most useful for conglomerates and holding companies that own multiple distinct businesses. For example, Infratil owns Vodafone NZ, Wellington Airport, Longroad Energy (US renewables), Qscan Group (radiology) and other assets. Each division has a different earnings profile and appropriate valuation multiple. SOTP values each division separately using appropriate methods, then adds them up (with adjustments for corporate overhead and holding company discount). For a focused single-business company like Mainfreight, a standard P/E or EV/EBITDA multiple approach is more appropriate than SOTP.

activities.question: An analyst forecasts FPH EPS of $0.62 next year and believes 38× is the appropriate P/E given FPH's growth profile. What is the 12-month price target?
activities.hint: Target price = forecast EPS × P/E multiple.
activities.explanation: $0.62 × 38 = $23.56. If FPH is currently trading at $21.40, this target implies approximately 10.1% upside over 12 months. The 38× multiple reflects FPH's consistent EPS growth (typically 10–20% per year), global market leadership and high quality earnings. At 38× it is at the high end of NZX multiples — justified if FPH's growth continues, but risky if growth slows.

activities.title: Your First Valuation
activities.prompt: Using the P/E method, estimate a 12-month price target for any NZ company you have been following. Find the current EPS (from any financial data source), apply a growth rate of your choice, and choose a P/E multiple based on sector peers. Show your working in three steps. How much upside or downside does your target imply from today's price?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — producing your first price target makes all the theory concrete. It will be wrong — all price targets are — but the process builds analytical discipline.
